question_text: A musician plays the same note (same frequency) on two different instruments: a flute and a violin. Both produce sound at 440 Hz, but the sounds have different timbres (qualities). Which statement best explains this difference using wave properties?

options.label: A
options.text: The flute produces a higher amplitude wave than the violin

options.label: B
options.text: The two instruments produce different combinations of the fundamental frequency and overtones with different amplitudes

options.label: C
options.text: The violin produces sound that travels faster through air than the flute

options.label: D
options.text: The flute and violin resonate at different frequencies, so they cannot produce the same note

correct_answer: The two instruments produce different combinations of the fundamental frequency and overtones with different amplitudes
explanation: Timbre (or tone quality) is what makes a flute sound different from a violin even when playing the same note. This difference comes from overtones—extra frequencies that sound alongside the main frequency (440 Hz). Each instrument produces different overtones at different loudnesses (amplitudes), which creates its unique sound character. The fundamental frequency is the same, but the harmonic content is different.
